title: Increasing and decreasing intervals
description: Highlight intervals on the domain of a function where it's only increasing or only decreasing.
topic_path: Intervals where a function is positive, negative, increasing, or decreasing

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select all the intervals where $f$ is decreasing.**



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$f$ is decreasing in an interval where as $x$ increases, $f(x)$ decreases.

Visually, this is where the graph is going downwards as we go from left to right.



[[☃ image 1]]

Now we can check each option:

Interval | Is $f$ decreasing on the interval?
:-: | :-:
$-5<x<-2.5$ | $\greenD{\text{Yes!}}$
$-2.5<x<1.75$ | $\redD{\text{No}}$. $f$ is increasing on this interval
$1.75<x<5$ | $\greenD{\text{Yes!}}$

options.alt: A coordinate plane. The x-axis scales by one, and the y-axis scales by eight. The graph of y equals f of x is a continuous curve. From left to right, it passes through the point negative five, forty and the point negative three point five, zero. Then, there's a local minimum at negative two point five, negative ten followed by an x-intercept at negative one, zero. The curve continues to the y-intercept zero, fourteen and a local maximum at one point seven-five, twenty-eight. The curve heads towards the point three point seven, zero, and four point five, negative 40. All of these values are estimated.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select all the intervals where $g$ is decreasing.**



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$g$ is decreasing in an interval where as $x$ increases, $g(x)$ decreases.

Visually, this is where the graph is going downwards as we go from left to right.



[[☃ image 1]]

Now we can check each option:

Interval | Is $g$ decreasing on the interval?
:-: | :-:
$-1<x<0$ | $\greenD{\text{Yes!}}$
$1<x<2$ | $\greenD{\text{Yes!}}$
$3<x<4$ | $\greenD{\text{Yes!}}$

options.alt: A coordinate plane. The x-axis scales by one, and the y-axis scales by four. The graph of y equals g of x is a continuous curve. From left to right, it passes through the point negative four, negative eight and the x-intercept negative two, zero. Then, there's a local maximum at negative one, one, then the curve decreases through the origin. The curve continues to two, negative eight and three, negative fifteen. All of these values are estimated.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select all the intervals where $h$ is decreasing.**



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$h$ is decreasing in an interval where as $x$ increases, $h(x)$ decreases.

Visually, this is where the graph is going downwards as we go from left to right.



[[☃ image 1]]

Now we can check each option:

Interval | Is $h$ decreasing on the interval?
:-: | :-:
$-4<x<-3$ | $\greenD{\text{Yes!}}$
$-2<x<0$ | $\redD{\text{No}}$. $h$ is increasing on this interval
$0<x<2$ | $\redD{\text{No}}$. $h$ is increasing on this interval

options.alt: A coordinate plane. The x-axis scales by one, and the y-axis scales by ten. The graph of y equals h of x is a continuous curve. From left to right, it passes through negative five, forty and the x-intercept negative four, zero. The function continues to decrease until the local minimum at negative two and one-half, negative twenty-five. Then it increases through the origin until the local maximum at two and one-half, twenty five. The function then decreases again through the x-intercept four, zero and goes through the point five, negative forty. All values are estimates.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select all the intervals where $f$ is decreasing.**



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$f$ is decreasing in an interval where as $x$ increases, $f(x)$ decreases.

Visually, this is where the graph is going downwards as we go from left to right.



[[☃ image 1]]

Now we can check each option:

Interval | Is $f$ decreasing on the interval?
:-: | :-:
$-4.5<x<-4$ | $\redD{\text{No}}$. $f$ is increasing on this interval
$-0.5<x<0$ | $\redD{\text{No}}$. $f$ is increasing on this interval
$1<x<2$ | $\redD{\text{No}}$. $f$ is increasing on this interval

Therefore, $f$ is decreasing on none of the above intervals.

options.alt: A coordinate plane. The x-axis scales by one, and the y-axis scales by ten. The graph of y equals f of x is a continuous curve. From left to right, it passes through the point negative five, negative forty-five and the x-intercept negative four and one-half, zero. It continues to increase until the local maximum at negative three and one-half, twenty-eight. Then it decreases through the x-intercept negative two and one-quarter, zero until a local minimum at negative one, negative twenty. Then it increases through the origin and the point one, forty. It continues to increase until the local maximum at two and one-half, ninety. Then it decreases through the point three, seventy and the x-intercept three and three-quarters, zero.

From left to right, it passes through the point negative five, negative ten and decreases through negative four, negative fifty until it reaches a local minimum at negative two and one-half, negative sixty-five. Then it increases through the point negative one, negative fifty and the y-intercept zero, negative twenty-five. It continues to increases until the local maximum at two and one-half, twelve. Then it decreases through the point four, zero and the point five, negative forty. All values are estimates.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select all the intervals where $g$ is increasing.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:

Visually, this is where the graph is going upwards as we go from left to right.

Now we can check each option:

Interval | Is $g$ increasing on the interval?
:-: | :-:
$-5<x<-2$ | $\redD{\text{No}}$. $g$ is decreasing on this interval
$-2<x<0$ | $\redD{\text{No}}$. $g$ is decreasing on this interval
$2<x<5$ | $\greenD{\text{Yes!}}$

options.alt: A coordinate plane. The x-axis scales by one, and the y-axis scales by four. The graph of y equals g of x is a continuous curve. From left to right, it passes through the point negative four, fourteen and the x-intercept negative two, zero. It continues to decrease through the y-intercept zero, negative five and one-half until the local minimum at one-half, negative six. Then it increases through the point two, negative four, the x-intercept three, zero and the point four, six. All values are estimated.
